back: A square matrix A is diagonalizable if there exists an invertible matrix P and a diagonal matrix D such that P⁻¹AP = D, where D contains eigenvalues of A on its diagonal.

front: What is the condition for a matrix to be diagonalizable?
back: A matrix A of order n is diagonalizable if it has n linearly independent eigenvectors. Equivalently, for each eigenvalue, geometric multiplicity equals algebraic multiplicity.
For a square matrix A, a non-zero vector v is an eigenvector if Av = λv for some scalar λ (the eigenvalue). The eigenvector direction remains unchanged by the linear transformation.
Two matrices A and B are similar if there exists an invertible matrix P such that B = P⁻¹AP. Similar matrices represent the same linear transformation in different bases.
A matrix A can be orthogonally diagonalized if there exists an orthogonal matrix Q such that Q⁻¹AQ = QᵀAQ = D (diagonal). This is possible if and only if A is symmetric.
For any symmetric matrix A, there exists an orthogonal matrix Q such that QᵀAQ = D, where D is a diagonal matrix containing eigenvalues of A. The columns of Q are orthonormal eigenvectors.

front: What is the difference between algebraic and geometric multiplicity?
back: Algebraic multiplicity is the number of times an eigenvalue appears as a root of the characteristic polynomial. Geometric multiplicity is the dimension of the eigenspace (number of linearly independent eigenvectors for that eigenvalue).

front: How to find P and D in diagonalization?
back: 1) Find eigenvalues from characteristic polynomial 2) Find eigenvectors for each eigenvalue 3) Form P with eigenvectors as columns 4) D has eigenvalues on diagonal corresponding to eigenvector columns in P.
A diagonal matrix has all non-zero elements only on the main diagonal (aᵢⱼ = 0 for i ≠ j). It simplifies matrix operations significantly.

front: How to compute Aⁿ using diagonalization?
back: If A = PDP⁻¹, then Aⁿ = PDⁿP⁻¹. Since D is diagonal with eigenvalues λᵢ, Dⁿ is diagonal with λᵢⁿ on the diagonal, making computation easy.
A matrix Q is orthogonal if Q⁻¹ = Qᵀ. Its columns are orthonormal vectors, meaning QᵀQ = I. Orthogonal matrices preserve length and angles.
A symmetric matrix satisfies A = Aᵀ (aᵢⱼ = aⱼᵢ). All eigenvalues of a symmetric matrix are real, and eigenvectors corresponding to distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal.
The characteristic equation is det(A - λI) = 0. Its roots are the eigenvalues of matrix A.
The eigenspace corresponding to eigenvalue λ is the set of all eigenvectors plus the zero vector: {v | (A - λI)v = 0}. Its dimension is the geometric multiplicity.
A matrix cannot be diagonalized if it lacks enough linearly independent eigenvectors. Example: [1 1; 0 1] has eigenvalue 1 with algebraic multiplicity 2 but only one eigenvector.
